content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 > [!IMPORTANT] 4 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** 5 > 6 > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a three-container security apparatus: a privileged pentacle establishes the network namespace, a sentry enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the bottle workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. 7 > 8 > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 9 10 Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation. 11 12 On the build side, Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 13 14 On the runtime side, Recipe Bottle interposes a sentry container between untrusted workloads and system resources, enforcing network policy via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` — without requiring modifications to existing container images. 15 16 The system uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, and `docker` natively. No `gcloud` CLI is required on your workstation — cloud operations use REST APIs via `curl` and `jq`. 17 18 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 19 20

21 22

23 24 ## Key Concepts 25 26 | Term | Meaning | 27 |------|---------| 28 | **Vessel** | A specification for a container image — built from source ([conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([graft](#Graft)) | 29 | **Ark** | An immutable container image artifact in the registry, produced from a [vessel](#Vessel) | 30 | **Hallmark** | A specific build instance of a [vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp | 31 | **Vouch** | Cryptographic attestation proving an [ark](#Ark) was built by trusted infrastructure | 32 | **Depot** | The facility where container images are built and stored (GCP project + registry + bucket) | 33 | **Nameplate** | Per-vessel configuration tying a [sentry](#Sentry) and [bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable unit | 34 | **Sentry** | Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` | 35 | **Pentacle** | Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [bottle](#Bottle) | 36 | **Bottle** | Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment | 37 | **Charge** | Start the [sentry](#Sentry)/[pentacle](#Pentacle)/[bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [nameplate](#Nameplate) | 38 | **Quench** | Stop and clean up a charged [nameplate](#Nameplate)'s containers | 39 | **Ordain** | Create a [hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build command | 40 | **Conjure** | [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: Cloud Build creates the image from source | 41 | **Bind** | [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: mirror an upstream image pinned by digest | 42 | **Graft** | [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: push a locally-built image to the registry | 43 | **Kludge** | Build a [vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration (no registry push) | 44 | **Enshrine** | Mirror upstream base images into your [depot](#Depot)'s registry | 45 | **Summon** | Pull a [hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [depot](#Depot) to your local machine | 46 | **Plumb** | Inspect an artifact's provenance (SBOM, build info, [vouch](#Vouch) chain) | 47 | **Tally** | Inventory [hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the registry by health status | 48 | **Levy** | Provision a new [depot](#Depot)'s GCP infrastructure | 49 | **Payor** | Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth | 50 | **Governor** | Administers a [depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access | 51 | **Director** | Builds and publishes [vessel](#Vessel) images into a [depot](#Depot) | 52 | **Retriever** | Pulls and runs [vessel](#Vessel) images from a [depot](#Depot) | 53 | **Charter** | Create a [retriever](#Retriever) service account ([governor](#Governor) operation) | 54 | **Knight** | Create a [director](#Director) service account ([governor](#Governor) operation) | 55 56 ## How It Works 57 58 ### Image Management 59 60 Recipe Bottle builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 61 62 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 63 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 64 - SLSA provenance attestation and verification 65 - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build 66 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 67 68 ### Bottle Orchestration 69 70 For running containers with network services, Recipe Bottle orchestrates three containers working together: 71 72 - **Sentry** — enforces network security policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` 73 - **Pentacle** — establishes a privileged network namespace and shares it with the bottle 74 - **Bottle** — your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment 75 76 This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the bottle container experiences only a functional path to its sentry gateway. 77 78 ## Prerequisites 79 80 - macOS or Linux workstation 81 - `bash` (3.2+), `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` 82 - `docker` (container runtime) 83 - A Google Cloud account with billing enabled (credit card required for verification; free tier is sufficient to start) 84 85 ## Using the CLI 86 87 All Recipe Bottle operations are **tabtargets** — lightweight shell scripts in the `tt/` directory. Run them from the project root. 88 89 **Discover commands**: `ls tt/` shows everything. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-`. 90 91 **Naming pattern**: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh` 92 - **Colophon**: routing identifier (e.g. `rbw-cC`) 93 - **Frontispiece**: human-readable description (e.g. `Start`) 94 - **Imprint**: optional target parameter, often a vessel name (e.g. `tadmor`) 95 96 Example: `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` charges the `tadmor` crucible. 97 98 ## Setup 99 100 Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles: 101 102 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 103 |------|-------------------|---------| 104 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 105 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [director](#Director) and [retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [depot](#Depot) | 106 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | 107 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 108 109 The payor stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 110 111 ### Onboarding Guide 112 113 The onboarding guide detects which roles you have credentials for and routes you to the right walkthrough: 114 115 ``` 116 tt/rbw-go.OnboardMAIN.sh 117 ``` 118 119 Each role has its own walkthrough that probes your progress and shows the next step. For a full health dashboard across all roles: 120 121 ``` 122 tt/rbw-gOr.OnboardReference.sh 123 ``` 124 125 ### Phase 1: Payor Establishment 126 127 This phase involves manual work in the Google Cloud Console: creating a GCP project, enabling APIs, and configuring an OAuth consent screen. The guided procedure walks you through each click. 128 129 1. **Configure payor project** — Edit `.rbk/rbrp.env` and set `RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID` to your desired GCP project ID (must be globally unique across all of GCP, 6-30 characters, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens). 130 131 2. **Establish payor** — Follow the guided procedure to create the GCP project and configure OAuth consent screen: 132 ``` 133 tt/rbw-gPE.PayorEstablish.sh 134 ``` 135 This displays step-by-step instructions for the Google Cloud Console. At the end you will download a JSON key file containing OAuth client credentials. 136 137 3. **Install OAuth credentials** — Ingest the JSON key file downloaded during establishment. A browser window will open for authorization: 138 ``` 139 tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_*.json 140 ``` 141 On success, the refresh token is stored in `~/.rbw/rbro.env` with `600` permissions. You will not need to repeat the browser flow. 142 143 ### Phase 2: Infrastructure Provisioning 144 145 5. **Review depot configuration** — Edit `.rbk/rbrr.env` to set region, machine type, and other depot parameters. Use the render command to review: 146 ``` 147 tt/rbw-rrr.RenderRepoRegime.sh 148 ``` 149 150 6. **Levy depot** — This provisions the GCP depot project with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets. This binds your RBRR configuration to real cloud resources: 151 ``` 152 tt/rbw-dL.PayorLeviesDepot.sh 153 ``` 154 155 7. **Mantle governor** — Admin service account for the depot: 156 ``` 157 tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh 158 ``` 159 160 ### Phase 3: Credential Creation (Governor role) 161 162 8. **Knight director** — Build service account. The instance name labels this director — use a short identifier: 163 ``` 164 tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh 165 ``` 166 167 9. **Charter retriever** — Image pull service account. Use the same instance name as your director: 168 ``` 169 tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh 170 ``` 171 172 ### Phase 4: Build & Retrieve (Director + Retriever roles) 173 174 10. **Ordain hallmark** — Build (conjure) or mirror (bind) each vessel's image (typically 10-20 minutes for conjure builds): 175 ``` 176 tt/rbw-hO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-vessels/ 177 ``` 178 179 13. **Check & vouch** — Verify builds completed and SLSA provenance: 180 ``` 181 tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh 182 tt/rbw-hV.DirectorVouchesHallmarks.sh 183 ``` 184 185 14. **Record hallmarks** — Copy the hallmark values from the check output into your nameplate regime file: 186 ``` 187 # Edit .rbk/rbrn_.env and set: 188 RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=c260101120000-r260101130000 189 RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=c260101120000-r260101140000 190 ``` 191 192 15. **Summon** — Pull vouched images locally (Retriever role): 193 ``` 194 tt/rbw-hs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh 195 ``` 196 197 ## Day-to-Day Operations 198 199 The examples below use `tadmor` (the included test nameplate). Replace with your own nameplate moniker — imprints in tabtarget filenames match the nameplate name from `.rbk/*/rbrn.env`. 200 201 ### Starting a Bottle 202 203 ``` 204 tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh 205 ``` 206 207 This charges the crucible — starts the sentry, pentacle, and bottle containers for the vessel. 208 209 ### Diagnostics 210 211 ``` 212 tt/rbw-cr.Rack.sh tadmor # Shell into bottle (rack the demon) 213 tt/rbw-ch.Hail.sh tadmor # Shell into sentry (hail the guard) 214 tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh tadmor # Observe network traffic (scry the topology) 215 ``` 216 217 ### Stopping 218 219 ``` 220 tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh 221 ``` 222 223 ### Plumbing Provenance 224 225 ``` 226 tt/rbw-hpf.RetrieverPlumbsFull.sh # Full: SBOM, build info, Dockerfile 227 tt/rbw-hpc.RetrieverPlumbsCompact.sh # Compact summary 228 ``` 229 230 ## Credential Safety 231 232 All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control. 233 234 | Credential | Location | Created by | 235 |------------|----------|------------| 236 | Payor OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` | 237 | Governor | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh` | 238 | Director | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh` | 239 | Retriever | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh` | 240 241 Each credential file is scoped to one role within one depot and cannot operate outside its designation. 242 243 ## Configuration 244 245 Recipe Bottle uses a Config Regime system — structured configuration with typed validation. Each regime has a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness). 246 247 ### User-Configured Regimes 248 249 | Regime | File | Purpose | Render | Validate | 250 |--------|------|---------|--------|----------| 251 | RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | Payor GCP project identity | `tt/rbw-rpr.*` | `tt/rbw-rpv.*` | 252 | RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | Depot project, region, build config | `tt/rbw-rrr.*` | `tt/rbw-rrv.*` | 253 | RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-vessel: runtime, hallmarks | `tt/rbw-rnr.*` | `tt/rbw-rnv.*` | 254 | RBRV | vessel dirs | Container image build definitions | `tt/rbw-rvr.*` | `tt/rbw-rvv.*` | 255 | RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | `tt/rbw-rsr.*` | `tt/rbw-rsv.*` | 256 257 ### Managed Regimes (generated by commands) 258 259 | Regime | Purpose | Generated by | 260 |--------|---------|-------------| 261 | RBRO | OAuth refresh token | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` | 262 | RBRA | Service account credentials | `tt/rbw-aM.*` / `tt/rbw-aK.*` / `tt/rbw-aC.*` | 263 264 ### BUK Base Regimes 265 266 | Regime | File | Purpose | 267 |--------|------|---------| 268 | BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure (tabtarget dir, tools dir) | 269 | BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. | 270 271 ## Vessels and Nameplates 272 273 **Vessels** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`: 274 275 ``` 276 rbev-vessels/ 277 ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/ # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source) 278 │ ├── Dockerfile 279 │ └── rbrv.env 280 ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/ # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source) 281 │ ├── Dockerfile 282 │ └── rbrv.env 283 └── rbev-bottle-plantuml/ # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest) 284 └── rbrv.env 285 ``` 286 287 Conjure vessels have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. Bind vessels (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. Graft vessels push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and vouch still run in Cloud Build. The same `tt/rbw-hO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh` command handles all three: it detects the vessel mode and triggers a Cloud Build (conjure), mirrors from upstream (bind), or pushes a local image (graft). Trust hierarchy: conjure has full SLSA provenance, bind has digest-pin verification, graft has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict). 288 289 **Nameplates** tie vessels together into a runnable bottle. The nameplate moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) is what appears as the imprint in tabtarget filenames: 290 291 ``` 292 .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit 293 ``` 294 295 So `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` charges the crucible defined by the `tadmor` nameplate, which selects its sentry and bottle vessel images. 296 297 ## Testing 298 299 Run test fixtures **sequentially** — they share regime state and container namespaces. Never run fixtures in parallel. 300 301 ``` 302 tt/rbtd-r.Run.regime-validation.sh 303 tt/rbtd-r.Run.tadmor.sh 304 ``` 305 306 List all available fixtures and suites: 307 ``` 308 ls tt/rbtd-r.Run.* 309 ls tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.* 310 ``` 311 312 Qualification gates: 313 ``` 314 tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh # Fast: tabtargets, colophons, nameplate health 315 tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh # Release: + shellcheck, full test suite 316 ``` 317 318 ## Recovery 319 320 - **Lost OAuth credentials**: Download a fresh JSON key from Google Cloud Console, re-run `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` 321 - **Expired tokens**: `tt/rbw-gPR.PayorRefresh.sh` 322 - **Compromised governor**: `tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh` (replaces service account, invalidates old credential) 323 - **Compromised director/retriever**: `tt/rbw-aF.GovernorForfeitsServiceAccount.sh` to revoke, then re-knight with `tt/rbw-aK.*` or re-charter with `tt/rbw-aC.*` 324 - **Lost nameplate values**: Re-run `tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh` to retrieve hallmark values from the registry 325 - **Build timeout or failure**: Check build status with `tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh`, review logs in the GCP Console for the depot project 326 327 ## Architecture 328 329 ``` 330 Project Root/ 331 ├── .buk/ # BUK launcher directory + BURC config 332 ├── .rbk/ # Recipe Bottle config regimes 333 ├── tt/ # TabTargets — all CLI commands live here 334 ├── Tools/ 335 │ ├── buk/ # Bash Utility Kit (portable CLI infrastructure) 336 │ └── rbk/ # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic) 337 └── rbev-vessels/ # Vessel definitions (Dockerfile + rbrv.env per vessel) 338 ``` 339 340 ## Claude Code 341 342 If you use [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code), the project includes a `CLAUDE.md` with a full command reference table, glossary, and conventions for AI-assisted development. 343 344 ## License 345 346 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 347 348 Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0. 349

# Recipe Bottle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** > > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a three-container security apparatus: a privileged pentacle establishes the network namespace, a sentry enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the bottle workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. > > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation. On the build side, Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. On the runtime side, Recipe Bottle interposes a sentry container between untrusted workloads and system resources, enforcing network policy via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` — without requiring modifications to existing container images. The system uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, and `docker` natively. No `gcloud` CLI is required on your workstation — cloud operations use REST APIs via `curl` and `jq`. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Key Concepts | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Vessel** | A specification for a container image — built from source ([conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([graft](#Graft)) | | **Ark** | An immutable container image artifact in the registry, produced from a [vessel](#Vessel) | | **Hallmark** | A specific build instance of a [vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp | | **Vouch** | Cryptographic attestation proving an [ark](#Ark) was built by trusted infrastructure | | **Depot** | The facility where container images are built and stored (GCP project + registry + bucket) | | **Nameplate** | Per-vessel configuration tying a [sentry](#Sentry) and [bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable unit | | **Sentry** | Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` | | **Pentacle** | Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [bottle](#Bottle) | | **Bottle** | Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment | | **Charge** | Start the [sentry](#Sentry)/[pentacle](#Pentacle)/[bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [nameplate](#Nameplate) | | **Quench** | Stop and clean up a charged [nameplate](#Nameplate)'s containers | | **Ordain** | Create a [hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build command | | **Conjure** | [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: Cloud Build creates the image from source | | **Bind** | [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: mirror an upstream image pinned by digest | | **Graft** | [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: push a locally-built image to the registry | | **Kludge** | Build a [vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration (no registry push) | | **Enshrine** | Mirror upstream base images into your [depot](#Depot)'s registry | | **Summon** | Pull a [hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [depot](#Depot) to your local machine | | **Plumb** | Inspect an artifact's provenance (SBOM, build info, [vouch](#Vouch) chain) | | **Tally** | Inventory [hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the registry by health status | | **Levy** | Provision a new [depot](#Depot)'s GCP infrastructure | | **Payor** | Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth | | **Governor** | Administers a [depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access | | **Director** | Builds and publishes [vessel](#Vessel) images into a [depot](#Depot) | | **Retriever** | Pulls and runs [vessel](#Vessel) images from a [depot](#Depot) | | **Charter** | Create a [retriever](#Retriever) service account ([governor](#Governor) operation) | | **Knight** | Create a [director](#Director) service account ([governor](#Governor) operation) | ## How It Works ### Image Management Recipe Bottle builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation - SLSA provenance attestation and verification - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts ### Bottle Orchestration For running containers with network services, Recipe Bottle orchestrates three containers working together: - **Sentry** — enforces network security policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` - **Pentacle** — establishes a privileged network namespace and shares it with the bottle - **Bottle** — your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the bottle container experiences only a functional path to its sentry gateway. ## Prerequisites - macOS or Linux workstation - `bash` (3.2+), `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` - `docker` (container runtime) - A Google Cloud account with billing enabled (credit card required for verification; free tier is sufficient to start) ## Using the CLI All Recipe Bottle operations are **tabtargets** — lightweight shell scripts in the `tt/` directory. Run them from the project root. **Discover commands**: `ls tt/` shows everything. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-`. **Naming pattern**: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh` - **Colophon**: routing identifier (e.g. `rbw-cC`) - **Frontispiece**: human-readable description (e.g. `Start`) - **Imprint**: optional target parameter, often a vessel name (e.g. `tadmor`) Example: `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` charges the `tadmor` crucible. ## Setup Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles: | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | |------|-------------------|---------| | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [governor](#Governor) lifecycle | | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [director](#Director) and [retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [depot](#Depot) | | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | The payor stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. ### Onboarding Guide The onboarding guide detects which roles you have credentials for and routes you to the right walkthrough: ``` tt/rbw-go.OnboardMAIN.sh ``` Each role has its own walkthrough that probes your progress and shows the next step. For a full health dashboard across all roles: ``` tt/rbw-gOr.OnboardReference.sh ``` ### Phase 1: Payor Establishment This phase involves manual work in the Google Cloud Console: creating a GCP project, enabling APIs, and configuring an OAuth consent screen. The guided procedure walks you through each click. 1. **Configure payor project** — Edit `.rbk/rbrp.env` and set `RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID` to your desired GCP project ID (must be globally unique across all of GCP, 6-30 characters, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens). 2. **Establish payor** — Follow the guided procedure to create the GCP project and configure OAuth consent screen: ``` tt/rbw-gPE.PayorEstablish.sh ``` This displays step-by-step instructions for the Google Cloud Console. At the end you will download a JSON key file containing OAuth client credentials. 3. **Install OAuth credentials** — Ingest the JSON key file downloaded during establishment. A browser window will open for authorization: ``` tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_*.json ``` On success, the refresh token is stored in `~/.rbw/rbro.env` with `600` permissions. You will not need to repeat the browser flow. ### Phase 2: Infrastructure Provisioning 5. **Review depot configuration** — Edit `.rbk/rbrr.env` to set region, machine type, and other depot parameters. Use the render command to review: ``` tt/rbw-rrr.RenderRepoRegime.sh ``` 6. **Levy depot** — This provisions the GCP depot project with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets. This binds your RBRR configuration to real cloud resources: ``` tt/rbw-dL.PayorLeviesDepot.sh ``` 7. **Mantle governor** — Admin service account for the depot: ``` tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh ``` ### Phase 3: Credential Creation (Governor role) 8. **Knight director** — Build service account. The instance name labels this director — use a short identifier: ``` tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh ``` 9. **Charter retriever** — Image pull service account. Use the same instance name as your director: ``` tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh ``` ### Phase 4: Build & Retrieve (Director + Retriever roles) 10. **Ordain hallmark** — Build (conjure) or mirror (bind) each vessel's image (typically 10-20 minutes for conjure builds): ``` tt/rbw-hO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-vessels/ ``` 13. **Check & vouch** — Verify builds completed and SLSA provenance: ``` tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh tt/rbw-hV.DirectorVouchesHallmarks.sh ``` 14. **Record hallmarks** — Copy the hallmark values from the check output into your nameplate regime file: ``` # Edit .rbk/rbrn_.env and set: RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=c260101120000-r260101130000 RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=c260101120000-r260101140000 ``` 15. **Summon** — Pull vouched images locally (Retriever role): ``` tt/rbw-hs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh ``` ## Day-to-Day Operations The examples below use `tadmor` (the included test nameplate). Replace with your own nameplate moniker — imprints in tabtarget filenames match the nameplate name from `.rbk/*/rbrn.env`. ### Starting a Bottle ``` tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh ``` This charges the crucible — starts the sentry, pentacle, and bottle containers for the vessel. ### Diagnostics ``` tt/rbw-cr.Rack.sh tadmor # Shell into bottle (rack the demon) tt/rbw-ch.Hail.sh tadmor # Shell into sentry (hail the guard) tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh tadmor # Observe network traffic (scry the topology) ``` ### Stopping ``` tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh ``` ### Plumbing Provenance ``` tt/rbw-hpf.RetrieverPlumbsFull.sh # Full: SBOM, build info, Dockerfile tt/rbw-hpc.RetrieverPlumbsCompact.sh # Compact summary ``` ## Credential Safety All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control. | Credential | Location | Created by | |------------|----------|------------| | Payor OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` | | Governor | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh` | | Director | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh` | | Retriever | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh` | Each credential file is scoped to one role within one depot and cannot operate outside its designation. ## Configuration Recipe Bottle uses a Config Regime system — structured configuration with typed validation. Each regime has a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness). ### User-Configured Regimes | Regime | File | Purpose | Render | Validate | |--------|------|---------|--------|----------| | RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | Payor GCP project identity | `tt/rbw-rpr.*` | `tt/rbw-rpv.*` | | RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | Depot project, region, build config | `tt/rbw-rrr.*` | `tt/rbw-rrv.*` | | RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-vessel: runtime, hallmarks | `tt/rbw-rnr.*` | `tt/rbw-rnv.*` | | RBRV | vessel dirs | Container image build definitions | `tt/rbw-rvr.*` | `tt/rbw-rvv.*` | | RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | `tt/rbw-rsr.*` | `tt/rbw-rsv.*` | ### Managed Regimes (generated by commands) | Regime | Purpose | Generated by | |--------|---------|-------------| | RBRO | OAuth refresh token | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` | | RBRA | Service account credentials | `tt/rbw-aM.*` / `tt/rbw-aK.*` / `tt/rbw-aC.*` | ### BUK Base Regimes | Regime | File | Purpose | |--------|------|---------| | BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure (tabtarget dir, tools dir) | | BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. | ## Vessels and Nameplates **Vessels** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`: ``` rbev-vessels/ ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/ # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source) │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── rbrv.env ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/ # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source) │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── rbrv.env └── rbev-bottle-plantuml/ # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest) └── rbrv.env ``` Conjure vessels have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. Trust hierarchy: conjure has full SLSA provenance, bind has digest-pin verification, graft has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict). **Nameplates** tie vessels together into a runnable bottle. The nameplate moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) is what appears as the imprint in tabtarget filenames: ``` .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit ``` So `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` charges the crucible defined by the `tadmor` nameplate, which selects its sentry and bottle vessel images. ## Testing Run test fixtures **sequentially** — they share regime state and container namespaces. Never run fixtures in parallel. ``` tt/rbtd-r.Run.regime-validation.sh tt/rbtd-r.Run.tadmor.sh ``` List all available fixtures and suites: ``` ls tt/rbtd-r.Run.* ls tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.* ``` Qualification gates: ``` tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh # Fast: tabtargets, colophons, nameplate health tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh # Release: + shellcheck, full test suite ``` ## Recovery - **Lost OAuth credentials**: Download a fresh JSON key from Google Cloud Console, re-run `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` - **Expired tokens**: `tt/rbw-gPR.PayorRefresh.sh` - **Compromised governor**: `tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh` (replaces service account, invalidates old credential) - **Compromised director/retriever**: `tt/rbw-aF.GovernorForfeitsServiceAccount.sh` to revoke, then re-knight with `tt/rbw-aK.*` or re-charter with `tt/rbw-aC.*` - **Lost nameplate values**: Re-run `tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh` to retrieve hallmark values from the registry - **Build timeout or failure**: Check build status with `tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh`, review logs in the GCP Console for the depot project ## Architecture ``` Project Root/ ├── .buk/ # BUK launcher directory + BURC config ├── .rbk/ # Recipe Bottle config regimes ├── tt/ # TabTargets — all CLI commands live here ├── Tools/ │ ├── buk/ # Bash Utility Kit (portable CLI infrastructure) │ └── rbk/ # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic) └── rbev-vessels/ # Vessel definitions (Dockerfile + rbrv.env per vessel) ``` ## Claude Code If you use [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code), the project includes a `CLAUDE.md` with a full command reference table, glossary, and conventions for AI-assisted development. ## License Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0.

1	// RBSCO-CosmologyIntro.adoc
2	//
3	// Recipe Bottle Cosmology Overview — the nosebleed-terse "what this is"
4	//
5	// PURPOSE: This document is the index.html frontmatter source for the
6	// Recipe Bottle project page (scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle).
7	//
8	// ANCESTRY: Renamed from index.adoc (last committed 84323c31, 2025-08-18).
9	// Full git lineage: 31 commits from 2642121c through 84323c31.
10	//
11	// DEPENDENCY: Requires RBS0-SpecTop.adoc mapping section for attribute resolution.
12	
13	== Overview
14	
15	The
16	{at_rbm_system}
17	enables developers to safely run untrusted containers -
18	a significantly distinct use case than typical container deployments of carefully crafted code.
19	
20	While containers excel at packaging known applications,
21	running third-party or experimental code poses security risks.
22	The
23	{at_rbm_system}
24	addresses this by interposing a security layer
25	({at_sentry_container})
26	between untrusted containers
27	({at_bottle_container_s})
28	and system resources,
29	without requiring modifications to existing container images.
30	
31	This approach allows developers to leverage the vast ecosystem of containerized tools while maintaining strict security boundaries -
32	essential for small teams experimenting with diverse, potentially risky codebases.
33	
34	== Accomplished Infrastructure
35	
36	The
37	{at_rbm_system}
38	migrated from GitHub Actions and GHCR to Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR) in August 2025.
39	GHCR violated OCI specification for reference counting, and GitHub Actions' shared runner environment enabled cache poisoning attacks — both unacceptable for building containers from untrusted code.
40	
41	GCB and GAR provide isolated build environments, SLSA Build Level 3 attestations (verified end-to-end March 2026), reference-counted layer management, and native vulnerability scanning.
42	The project's build infrastructure was converted from Makefiles to pure bash, and the container runtime switched from podman to docker in December 2025.
43	
44	== The {at_rbm_system} Vision
45	
46	.RBM System Architecture
47	image::rbm-abstract-drawio.svg[RBM Architecture,600,400,align="center"]
48	
49	Maintaining build and service environments is a headache for the small development organization.
50	{st_container_s}
51	are an amazing tool for controlling package version constellations.
52	Necessary, but insufficient; I find that I need more redundancy, security, and control for my build setups.
53	
54	The
55	{at_rbm_system}
56	is my answer for filling in the gaps.
57	The vision is simple: how can an
58	{at_operator}
59	run only a few apps natively on a
60	{at_workstation}
61	to coordinate and arrange
62	{st_container_s}
63	in sophisticated yet safe ways for me and my customer's work?
64	
65	{at_rbm_system} uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` and a
66	{at_runtime}
67	(currently `docker`) natively to produce a safe and controlled space for development involving
68	{st_container_s}
69	heavily.
70	{at_rbm_system}
71	itself is largely a set of bash scripts designed to be easily incorporated into arbitrary projects via
72	`git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`
73	graft.
74	This is purely an open-source undertaking.
75	
76	=== Part One: {st_image} Management
77	
78	The
79	{at_rbm_system}
80	streamlines
81	{st_image}
82	creation and curation through Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR).
83	The
84	{at_build_service}:
85	
86	- Constructs
87	{st_image_s}
88	in clean, isolated environments using **Google-curated Cloud Build builder images**
89	- **Replicates trusted external images** into GAR with digest preservation and provenance tracking
90	- Supports multi-architecture builds via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation
91	- Generates software bills of material for compliance
92	- Captures and stores full build transcripts for provenance
93	- Maintains build transcripts, SBOMs, and commit references in an **auxiliary metadata artifact** separate from the runtime image
94	- Provides local trust posture inspection ({rbtgo_ark_inspect}) with compact and full views of build provenance, SLSA attestations, SBOM contents, and vouch verification results
95	- Stores validated
96	{st_image_s}
97	in Google Artifact Registry, tagged with minimal non-invasive labels
98	
99	This dual approach - building custom images and importing vetted external ones - ensures all container images used in development have known provenance and consistent tagging.
100	Small teams can maintain a curated registry combining custom-built tools with carefully selected upstream images, all traceable through GAR's unified storage.
101	
102	The system uses only
103	`curl`,
104	`jq`,
105	and
106	`bash`
107	scripts
108	to orchestrate both remote
109	{st_image}
110	construction and registry-to-registry replication, enabling small teams to maintain enterprise-grade
111	{st_image}
112	management practices without complex tooling.
113	`gcloud` is never required on the
114	{at_workstation};
115	it appears only inside Google-managed Cloud Build step containers (e.g. `gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud`) where it runs server-side during remote builds.
116	
117	Auxiliary provenance data, including the per-platform SBOM (`sbom.linux_amd64.spdx.json`), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries, is bundled into a compressed archive and uploaded to GAR as a **Generic Artifact** using the image tag as the package name and a fixed `metadata` version identifier.
118	This design keeps the primary image minimal and broadly usable while making rich build history available to those who need it.
119	
120	=== Part Two: {rbsc_crucible} Orchestration
121	
122	For development services requiring internet and/or IP connectivity at the
123	{at_workstation},
124	{at_rbm_system}
125	then orchestrates startup and configuration of
126	{rbsc_crucible_s}, which are comprised of a
127	{at_sentry_container},
128	a
129	{at_pentacle_container},
130	and a
131	{at_bottle_container}
132	operating together.
133	
134	The {at_rbm_system}
135	allows any
136	{st_image}
137	providing or using network services to function as a
138	{at_bottle_container}.
139	The
140	{at_pentacle_container}
141	establishes a privileged network namespace, configures it to route all traffic through the
142	{at_sentry_container},
143	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
144	{at_bottle_container}.
145	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
146	{at_bottle_container}
147	experiences only a functional path to its
148	{at_sentry_container}
149	gateway.
150	
151	The
152	{at_sentry_container}
153	thus sets up a potentially sophisticated set of network security safeguards that prevent malicious or compromised
154	{at_bottle_container_s}
155	from exfiltrating the
156	{at_operator_p}
157	assets.
158	Through configuration of deeply mature tools `iptables` and `dnsmasq`, the
159	{at_sentry_container}
160	prevents such illegal accesses.
161	
162	By providing these controlled yet accessible tools, the
163	{at_rbm_system}
164	enables small development teams to maintain proper container hygiene throughout their workflow - from initial building through deployment and eventual cleanup.
165	This empowers organizations to leverage the expanding ecosystem of containerized development tools without requiring specialized DevOps expertise.
166	
167	== Project Direction
168	
169	The following themes represent future directions for Recipe Bottle, drawn from the project's horizon roadmap.
170	
171	**Build pipeline security hardening.**
172	Egress lockdown, VPC Service Controls, cosign image signing, and Binary Authorization are evaluated and consciously deferred.
173	Each has a defined trigger condition — they activate when compliance requirements, threat models, or deployment targets change.
174	Full analysis lives in the Cloud Build security posture documentation.
175	
176	**IAM identity infrastructure.**
177	Service account numeric IDs for faster propagation, universal read-back verification after IAM grants, and universal SA preflight checks.
178	These reduce retry frequency and strengthen grant correctness guarantees.
179	
180	**Container runtime expansion.**
181	Rootless deployment, macOS-native workflows, and alternative container runtimes beyond the current docker-on-Linux model.
182	
183	== Links
184	
185	Source repository: https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle
186	
187	Project page: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle
188	
189	== Significant Events
190

// RBSCO-CosmologyIntro.adoc
//
// Recipe Bottle Cosmology Overview — the nosebleed-terse "what this is"
//
// PURPOSE: This document is the index.html frontmatter source for the
// Recipe Bottle project page (scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle).
//
// ANCESTRY: Renamed from index.adoc (last committed 84323c31, 2025-08-18).
// Full git lineage: 31 commits from 2642121c through 84323c31.
//
// DEPENDENCY: Requires RBS0-SpecTop.adoc mapping section for attribute resolution.

== Overview

The
{at_rbm_system}
enables developers to safely run untrusted containers -
a significantly distinct use case than typical container deployments of carefully crafted code.

While containers excel at packaging known applications,
running third-party or experimental code poses security risks.
The
{at_rbm_system}
addresses this by interposing a security layer
({at_sentry_container})
between untrusted containers
({at_bottle_container_s})
and system resources,
without requiring modifications to existing container images.

This approach allows developers to leverage the vast ecosystem of containerized tools while maintaining strict security boundaries -
essential for small teams experimenting with diverse, potentially risky codebases.

== Accomplished Infrastructure

The
{at_rbm_system}
migrated from GitHub Actions and GHCR to Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR) in August 2025.
GHCR violated OCI specification for reference counting, and GitHub Actions' shared runner environment enabled cache poisoning attacks — both unacceptable for building containers from untrusted code.

GCB and GAR provide isolated build environments, SLSA Build Level 3 attestations (verified end-to-end March 2026), reference-counted layer management, and native vulnerability scanning.
The project's build infrastructure was converted from Makefiles to pure bash, and the container runtime switched from podman to docker in December 2025.

== The {at_rbm_system} Vision

.RBM System Architecture
image::rbm-abstract-drawio.svg[RBM Architecture,600,400,align="center"]

Maintaining build and service environments is a headache for the small development organization.
{st_container_s}
are an amazing tool for controlling package version constellations.
Necessary, but insufficient; I find that I need more redundancy, security, and control for my build setups.

The
{at_rbm_system}
is my answer for filling in the gaps.
The vision is simple: how can an
{at_operator}
run only a few apps natively on a
{at_workstation}
to coordinate and arrange
{st_container_s}
in sophisticated yet safe ways for me and my customer's work?

{at_rbm_system} uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` and a
{at_runtime}
(currently `docker`) natively to produce a safe and controlled space for development involving
{st_container_s}
heavily.
{at_rbm_system}
itself is largely a set of bash scripts designed to be easily incorporated into arbitrary projects via
`git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`
graft.
This is purely an open-source undertaking.

=== Part One: {st_image} Management

The
{at_rbm_system}
streamlines
{st_image}
creation and curation through Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR).
The
{at_build_service}:

- Constructs
{st_image_s}
in clean, isolated environments using **Google-curated Cloud Build builder images**
- **Replicates trusted external images** into GAR with digest preservation and provenance tracking
- Supports multi-architecture builds via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation
- Generates software bills of material for compliance
- Captures and stores full build transcripts for provenance
- Maintains build transcripts, SBOMs, and commit references in an **auxiliary metadata artifact** separate from the runtime image
- Provides local trust posture inspection ({rbtgo_ark_inspect}) with compact and full views of build provenance, SLSA attestations, SBOM contents, and vouch verification results
- Stores validated
{st_image_s}
in Google Artifact Registry, tagged with minimal non-invasive labels

This dual approach - building custom images and importing vetted external ones - ensures all container images used in development have known provenance and consistent tagging.
Small teams can maintain a curated registry combining custom-built tools with carefully selected upstream images, all traceable through GAR's unified storage.

The system uses only
`curl`,
`jq`,
and
`bash`
scripts
to orchestrate both remote
{st_image}
construction and registry-to-registry replication, enabling small teams to maintain enterprise-grade
{st_image}
management practices without complex tooling.
`gcloud` is never required on the
{at_workstation};
it appears only inside Google-managed Cloud Build step containers (e.g. `gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud`) where it runs server-side during remote builds.

Auxiliary provenance data, including the per-platform SBOM (`sbom.linux_amd64.spdx.json`), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries, is bundled into a compressed archive and uploaded to GAR as a **Generic Artifact** using the image tag as the package name and a fixed `metadata` version identifier.
This design keeps the primary image minimal and broadly usable while making rich build history available to those who need it.

=== Part Two: {rbsc_crucible} Orchestration

For development services requiring internet and/or IP connectivity at the
{at_workstation},
{at_rbm_system}
then orchestrates startup and configuration of
{rbsc_crucible_s}, which are comprised of a
{at_sentry_container},
a
{at_pentacle_container},
and a
{at_bottle_container}
operating together.

The {at_rbm_system}
allows any
{st_image}
providing or using network services to function as a
{at_bottle_container}.
The
{at_pentacle_container}
establishes a privileged network namespace, configures it to route all traffic through the
{at_sentry_container},
then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
{at_bottle_container}.
This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
{at_bottle_container}
experiences only a functional path to its
{at_sentry_container}
gateway.

The
{at_sentry_container}
thus sets up a potentially sophisticated set of network security safeguards that prevent malicious or compromised
{at_bottle_container_s}
from exfiltrating the
{at_operator_p}
assets.
Through configuration of deeply mature tools `iptables` and `dnsmasq`, the
{at_sentry_container}
prevents such illegal accesses.

By providing these controlled yet accessible tools, the
{at_rbm_system}
enables small development teams to maintain proper container hygiene throughout their workflow - from initial building through deployment and eventual cleanup.
This empowers organizations to leverage the expanding ecosystem of containerized development tools without requiring specialized DevOps expertise.

== Project Direction

The following themes represent future directions for Recipe Bottle, drawn from the project's horizon roadmap.

**Build pipeline security hardening.**
Egress lockdown, VPC Service Controls, cosign image signing, and Binary Authorization are evaluated and consciously deferred.
Each has a defined trigger condition — they activate when compliance requirements, threat models, or deployment targets change.
Full analysis lives in the Cloud Build security posture documentation.

**IAM identity infrastructure.**
Service account numeric IDs for faster propagation, universal read-back verification after IAM grants, and universal SA preflight checks.
These reduce retry frequency and strengthen grant correctness guarantees.

**Container runtime expansion.**
Rootless deployment, macOS-native workflows, and alternative container runtimes beyond the current docker-on-Linux model.

== Links

Source repository: https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle

Project page: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

== Significant Events

| July 27, 2025

content.text:
All three sources read. I've created a task list tracking the 9 steps (consolidated where mechanical steps pair naturally).

## Step 1 Draft — Intro paragraph

**Insertion point**: Between the SVG (line 22) and `## Key Concepts` (line 24). This positions the orientation prose as the last thing a newcomer reads before the glossary — framing *how* to read what follows.

**Source notes**: Pulls from RBSCO Overview (untrusted-code framing, containers-excel-but-risky angle) and the Vision header (incorporation model, small-team framing, "gcloud never on workstation"). Does NOT duplicate what's in the existing elaboration (lines 12, 14) or what will appear in the expanded How It Works (Step 4).

**Draft** (~15 lines, third person):

```markdown
Recipe Bottle treats two complexity domains as distinct, orthogonal concerns. The first is **image management**: producing container images from untrusted source with verifiable provenance, orchestrated on Google Cloud Build with SLSA attestations, SBOMs, and digest-pinned toolchains. The second is **crucible orchestration**: running those images — or any unmodified third-party image — behind a security apparatus that enforces network policy without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other.

The distinctive case Recipe Bottle addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. The bottle container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged pentacle, with all egress flowing through a sentry gateway configured via mature tools (`iptables`, `dnsmasq`).

Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`. It is a small-team tool: a developer with a Google Cloud account and a workstation can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise, and without running `gcloud` on the workstation at all.
```

**Rationale for the three beats**:
1. **Two-domains framing** — load-bearing for everything downstream; names the split that Step 3's bridge and Step 4's H3 expansion rely on
2. **Why it exists (untrusted code)** — the distinctive use case from RBSCO Overview, compressed
3. **Ergonomics and incorporation model** — from RBSCO Vision header, addresses the "who is this for" question

**Notes for your review**:
- Terms used before the glossary: *pentacle*, *sentry*, *bottle*. All defined immediately below in Key Concepts. Forward reference acceptable here since the glossary is literally the next section.
- No markdown links yet — cross-linkification is Step 6. This draft stays link-free so you can evaluate prose first.
- I've bolded "image management" and "crucible orchestration" as the domain names. These match the H3s after Step 2's rename.
- "A developer can adopt either domain alone" prefigures Step 3's bridge sentence. The two reinforce each other without duplicating.

Approve as-is, or shall I revise voice, scope, beat structure, or insertion point?

19:The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
56:- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
88:When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
91:dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
131:[Omitted long matching line]
185:BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
203:  # Source all dependencies
219:### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
221:The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
223:**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
235:  # Always source light dependencies
239:  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
258:- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
259:- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
260:- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
842:**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.
1096:# Avoid these (bash 3.2 compatibility)
1206:**Loops**: `set -e` does not reliably catch failures inside `for`/`while` loop bodies in bash 3.2. Every command in a loop body must have explicit error handling.
1253:Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2. Two patterns are safe; choose by whether you need the index.
1276:# ❌ Crashes under set -u in bash 3.2 when array is empty
1333:The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh` (via `BURD_BUK_DIR`). All other module and config sourcing happens inside the furnish function, after the `buc_doc_env` / `buc_doc_env_done` gate. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime dependencies.
1339:**Prefer indirect expansion for reading** — `${!name}` works in bash 3.2:
1371:These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1375:Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.
1379:These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.
1395:Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.
1397:The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.
1401:Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:
1407:**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.
1411:LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.
1413:The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"
1580:- [ ] Target bash 3.2 minimum
1603:- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` sources all other dependencies and config files
1649:In bash 3.2, there is no module system, no scope isolation, and no cleanup-on-exit guarantee. When fixture A kindles modules and fixture B kindles the same modules, the kindle guards (`Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED`) from fixture A block fixture B. Subshells solve this: all state — kindle guards, sourced functions, variables — dies at the `)` boundary.
1698:- Kindles modules, sources dependencies, sets configuration

content.content:
1365	## Command Dependency Discipline
1366	
1367	External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.
1368	
1369	### POSIX Utility Allowlist
1370	
1371	These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1372	
1373	`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`
1374	
1375	Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.
1376	
1377	### Evicted Utilities
1378	
1379	These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.
1380	
1381	| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
1382	|---------|-------------|-------|
1383	| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
1384	| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
1385	| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
1386	| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
1387	| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
1388	| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
1389	| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
1390	| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
1391	| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |
1392	
1393	### Declared Dependency Principle
1394	
1395	Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.
1396	
1397	The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.
1398	
1399	### Platform-Variant Command Guidance
1400	
1401	Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:
1402	
1403	- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
1404	- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
1405	- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid
1406	
1407	**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.
1408	
1409	### LLM Contributor Checkpoint
1410	
1411	LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.
1412	
1413	The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"
1414	
1415	---
1416	
1417	## Output and Messaging Patterns
1418	
1419	### Message Hierarchy
1420	
1421	buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
1422	buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
1423	command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
1424	buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr

file.content:
## Command Dependency Discipline

External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.

### POSIX Utility Allowlist

These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:

`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`

Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.

### Evicted Utilities

These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.

| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |

### Declared Dependency Principle

Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.

The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.

### Platform-Variant Command Guidance

Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:

- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid

**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.

### LLM Contributor Checkpoint

LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.

The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"

---

## Output and Messaging Patterns

### Message Hierarchy

buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr

Got BCG's workstation floor: bash 3.2 target, POSIX allowlist (chmod, cp, date, find, mkdir, etc.), declared deps `openssl` + existing `git`/`curl`/`openssh`/`jq`/`docker`. Explicit "no python runtime" framing. Now executing Steps 1-6.

**Edit 1: Step 1 intro** (after SVG, before Key Concepts)

input.old_string: ## Key Concepts
input.new_string: Recipe Bottle treats two complexity domains as distinct, orthogonal concerns. The first is **image management**: producing container images from untrusted source with verifiable provenance. Builds run on Google Cloud Build in an egress-locked ("airgap") configuration using digest-pinned toolchains, produce SLSA attestations and SBOMs, and draw from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — so the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. The second domain is **crucible orchestration**: running those images — or any unmodified third-party image — behind a security apparatus that enforces network policy without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. The distinctive case Recipe Bottle addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. The [bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through a [sentry](#Sentry) gateway configured via the mature tools `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. ## Key Concepts
